12 Capstone — Newsroom Data Dashboard

← Back to lessons

Time to build something real. This capstone combines every Django concept from the course into a Newsroom Data Dashboard — a full-stack app that manages stories, datasets, sources, and reporters. The kind of internal tool a broadcast TV network would actually use.

Project Overview

You're building an internal newsroom dashboard where reporters can submit stories, upload datasets (CSV files, spreadsheets), track sources, and editors can manage everything with role-based permissions.

What You're Building
  • Story submission and management
  • Dataset upload and preview
  • Source tracking and verification
  • Reporter profiles and assignments
  • Editor dashboard with permissions
  • Search across stories and datasets
  • REST API for external tools
Django Concepts Used
  • Models with relationships (FK, M2M)
  • Class-based and function-based views
  • Templates with inheritance
  • Forms and ModelForms
  • Auth, permissions, groups
  • Django REST Framework
  • File uploads, search, pagination

Why a newsroom dashboard? This is the type of internal tool that broadcast TV networks (like Tenga) actually build. Election results, weather data, census figures — newsrooms need to ingest, manage, and publish data constantly. This project demonstrates you can build production tools, not just tutorials.

Phase 1 — Project Setup

# Create project and apps
django-admin startproject newsroom
cd newsroom
python manage.py startapp stories
python manage.py startapp datasets
python manage.py startapp accounts
python manage.py startapp api
newsroom/
  manage.py
  newsroom/ .............. project settings
    settings.py
    urls.py .............. root URL config
  stories/ ............... story management app
    models.py .......... Story, Source models
    views.py ........... list, detail, create views
    forms.py ........... story submission form
    urls.py
  datasets/ .............. dataset upload app
    models.py .......... Dataset model
    views.py ........... upload, preview views
    urls.py
  accounts/ .............. auth & profiles
    models.py .......... Reporter profile
    views.py
  api/ ................... DRF endpoints
    serializers.py
    views.py
    urls.py
  templates/ ............. all HTML templates
    base.html
    stories/
    datasets/
    accounts/ 
# settings.py — register your apps
INSTALLED_APPS = [
    'django.contrib.admin',
    'django.contrib.auth',
    'django.contrib.contenttypes',
    'django.contrib.sessions',
    'django.contrib.messages',
    'django.contrib.staticfiles',
    # Third-party
    'rest_framework',
    # Your apps
    'stories',
    'datasets',
    'accounts',
    'api',
]

AUTH_USER_MODEL = 'accounts.Reporter'  # custom user model
LOGIN_URL = '/accounts/login/'
LOGIN_REDIRECT_URL = '/dashboard/'
MEDIA_URL = '/media/'
MEDIA_ROOT = BASE_DIR / 'media'

Set AUTH_USER_MODEL BEFORE your first migration. If you run migrate before setting the custom user model, you'll have to delete the database and all migration files to change it later. Always define your custom user model first.

Phase 2 — Models

Reporter Model (Custom User)

# accounts/models.py
from django.contrib.auth.models import AbstractUser
from django.db import models

class Reporter(AbstractUser):
    """Custom user model for newsroom staff."""
    ROLE_CHOICES = [
        ('reporter', 'Reporter'),
        ('editor', 'Editor'),
        ('admin', 'Admin'),
    ]
    role = models.CharField(max_length=20, choices=ROLE_CHOICES, default='reporter')
    beat = models.CharField(max_length=100, blank=True, help_text="e.g., Politics, Weather, Crime")
    bio = models.TextField(blank=True)
    phone = models.CharField(max_length=20, blank=True)

    def __str__(self):
        return f"{self.get_full_name()} ({self.role})"

    @property
    def is_editor(self):
        return self.role in ('editor', 'admin')

Story & Source Models

# stories/models.py
from django.db import models
from django.conf import settings
from django.utils.text import slugify

class Source(models.Model):
    """A person or organization cited in stories."""
    name = models.CharField(max_length=200)
    title = models.CharField(max_length=200, blank=True, help_text="Job title or role")
    organization = models.CharField(max_length=200, blank=True)
    contact_info = models.TextField(blank=True, help_text="Phone, email — private field")
    is_verified = models.BooleanField(default=False)
    created_by = models.ForeignKey(settings.AUTH_USER_MODEL, on_delete=models.SET_NULL, null=True)

    def __str__(self):
        return f"{self.name} ({self.organization})" if self.organization else self.name

class Story(models.Model):
    STATUS_CHOICES = [
        ('draft', 'Draft'),
        ('review', 'In Review'),
        ('published', 'Published'),
        ('killed', 'Killed'),
    ]
    title = models.CharField(max_length=300)
    slug = models.SlugField(max_length=300, unique=True, blank=True)
    body = models.TextField()
    summary = models.TextField(max_length=500, blank=True, help_text="Brief summary for listings")
    status = models.CharField(max_length=20, choices=STATUS_CHOICES, default='draft')
    author = models.ForeignKey(settings.AUTH_USER_MODEL, on_delete=models.CASCADE, related_name='stories')
    sources = models.ManyToManyField(Source, blank=True, related_name='stories')
    created_at = models.DateTimeField(auto_now_add=True)
    updated_at = models.DateTimeField(auto_now=True)
    published_at = models.DateTimeField(null=True, blank=True)

    class Meta:
        verbose_name_plural = 'stories'
        ordering = ['-created_at']

    def __str__(self):
        return self.title

    def save(self, *args, **kwargs):
        if not self.slug:
            self.slug = slugify(self.title)
        super().save(*args, **kwargs)

Dataset Model

# datasets/models.py
from django.db import models
from django.conf import settings

class Dataset(models.Model):
    FORMAT_CHOICES = [
        ('csv', 'CSV'),
        ('xlsx', 'Excel'),
        ('json', 'JSON'),
        ('geojson', 'GeoJSON'),
        ('other', 'Other'),
    ]
    name = models.CharField(max_length=200)
    description = models.TextField(blank=True)
    file = models.FileField(upload_to='datasets/%Y/%m/')
    file_format = models.CharField(max_length=10, choices=FORMAT_CHOICES, default='csv')
    row_count = models.PositiveIntegerField(null=True, blank=True)
    source_url = models.URLField(blank=True, help_text="Original data source URL")
    uploaded_by = models.ForeignKey(settings.AUTH_USER_MODEL, on_delete=models.CASCADE)
    stories = models.ManyToManyField('stories.Story', blank=True, related_name='datasets')
    created_at = models.DateTimeField(auto_now_add=True)

    def __str__(self):
        return f"{self.name} ({self.file_format})"

    @property
    def file_size_display(self):
        """Human-readable file size."""
        size = self.file.size
        for unit in ['B', 'KB', 'MB', 'GB']:
            if size < 1024:
                return f"{size:.1f} {unit}"
            size /= 1024
        return f"{size:.1f} TB"

Model relationships recap: ForeignKey = one-to-many (a Story has one author, an author has many stories). ManyToManyField = many-to-many (a Story can have many Sources, a Source can appear in many Stories). related_name lets you query the reverse: reporter.stories.all() instead of Story.objects.filter(author=reporter).

# Run migrations
python manage.py makemigrations accounts stories datasets
python manage.py migrate
python manage.py createsuperuser

Phase 3 — Views

Story Views

# stories/views.py
from django.shortcuts import render, get_object_or_404, redirect
from django.contrib.auth.decorators import login_required
from django.db.models import Q
from django.core.paginator import Paginator
from .models import Story
from .forms import StoryForm

def story_list(request):
    """Public story listing — only published stories."""
    stories = Story.objects.filter(status='published').select_related('author')
    paginator = Paginator(stories, 20)
    page = paginator.get_page(request.GET.get('page'))
    return render(request, 'stories/list.html', {'page': page})

def story_detail(request, slug):
    """Single story view."""
    story = get_object_or_404(Story, slug=slug, status='published')
    return render(request, 'stories/detail.html', {'story': story})

@login_required
def story_create(request):
    """Create a new story — reporters only."""
    if request.method == 'POST':
        form = StoryForm(request.POST)
        if form.is_valid():
            story = form.save(commit=False)
            story.author = request.user
            story.save()
            form.save_m2m()  # save ManyToMany fields after commit=False
            return redirect('story-detail', slug=story.slug)
    else:
        form = StoryForm()
    return render(request, 'stories/create.html', {'form': form})

def story_search(request):
    """Search stories by title, body, or author."""
    query = request.GET.get('q', '')
    results = Story.objects.none()
    if query:
        results = Story.objects.filter(
            Q(title__icontains=query) |
            Q(body__icontains=query) |
            Q(author__first_name__icontains=query) |
            Q(author__last_name__icontains=query),
            status='published'
        ).select_related('author')
    return render(request, 'stories/search.html', {
        'query': query,
        'results': results,
    })

Q objects are Django's query combinators. Q(title__icontains=query) | Q(body__icontains=query) is like SQL's WHERE title LIKE '%..%' OR body LIKE '%..%'. You can combine with | (OR), & (AND), and ~ (NOT). It's Django's way of building search queries without raw SQL.

Dashboard View (Editor)

# stories/views.py (continued)
from django.contrib.auth.decorators import login_required, user_passes_test
from django.utils import timezone

def is_editor(user):
    return user.is_editor

@login_required
@user_passes_test(is_editor)
def editor_dashboard(request):
    """Editor dashboard — see all stories across the newsroom."""
    stories_in_review = Story.objects.filter(status='review').select_related('author')
    recent_drafts = Story.objects.filter(status='draft').order_by('-updated_at')[:10]
    published_today = Story.objects.filter(
        status='published',
        published_at__date=timezone.now().date()
    )
    return render(request, 'stories/dashboard.html', {
        'in_review': stories_in_review,
        'drafts': recent_drafts,
        'published_today': published_today,
    })

Dataset Views

# datasets/views.py
import csv
import io
from django.shortcuts import render, get_object_or_404, redirect
from django.contrib.auth.decorators import login_required
from .models import Dataset
from .forms import DatasetUploadForm

@login_required
def dataset_upload(request):
    if request.method == 'POST':
        form = DatasetUploadForm(request.POST, request.FILES)
        if form.is_valid():
            dataset = form.save(commit=False)
            dataset.uploaded_by = request.user
            # Count rows if CSV
            if dataset.file_format == 'csv':
                file_content = dataset.file.read().decode('utf-8')
                reader = csv.reader(io.StringIO(file_content))
                dataset.row_count = sum(1 for _ in reader) - 1  # minus header
                dataset.file.seek(0)  # reset file pointer
            dataset.save()
            form.save_m2m()
            return redirect('dataset-detail', pk=dataset.pk)
    else:
        form = DatasetUploadForm()
    return render(request, 'datasets/upload.html', {'form': form})

@login_required
def dataset_preview(request, pk):
    """Preview first 50 rows of a CSV dataset."""
    dataset = get_object_or_404(Dataset, pk=pk)
    preview_data = {'headers': [], 'rows': []}
    if dataset.file_format == 'csv':
        dataset.file.open('r')
        content = dataset.file.read().decode('utf-8')
        reader = csv.reader(io.StringIO(content))
        preview_data['headers'] = next(reader)
        preview_data['rows'] = [row for _, row in zip(range(50), reader)]
        dataset.file.close()
    return render(request, 'datasets/preview.html', {
        'dataset': dataset,
        'preview': preview_data,
    })

Phase 4 — Forms

# stories/forms.py
from django import forms
from .models import Story, Source

class StoryForm(forms.ModelForm):
    class Meta:
        model = Story
        fields = ['title', 'body', 'summary', 'status', 'sources']
        widgets = {
            'title': forms.TextInput(attrs={
                'class': 'form-input',
                'placeholder': 'Story headline...',
            }),
            'body': forms.Textarea(attrs={
                'class': 'form-textarea',
                'rows': 20,
                'placeholder': 'Write your story...',
            }),
            'summary': forms.Textarea(attrs={
                'class': 'form-textarea',
                'rows': 3,
                'placeholder': 'Brief summary for listings...',
            }),
            'sources': forms.CheckboxSelectMultiple(),
        }

    def clean_title(self):
        title = self.cleaned_data['title']
        if len(title) < 10:
            raise forms.ValidationError("Headlines must be at least 10 characters.")
        return title

    def clean_status(self):
        status = self.cleaned_data['status']
        # Reporters can't publish directly — only editors
        if status == 'published' and not self.initial.get('can_publish'):
            raise forms.ValidationError("Only editors can set status to Published.")
        return status
# datasets/forms.py
from django import forms
from .models import Dataset

class DatasetUploadForm(forms.ModelForm):
    class Meta:
        model = Dataset
        fields = ['name', 'description', 'file', 'file_format', 'source_url', 'stories']
        widgets = {
            'name': forms.TextInput(attrs={'class': 'form-input', 'placeholder': 'Dataset name...'}),
            'description': forms.Textarea(attrs={'class': 'form-textarea', 'rows': 4}),
            'stories': forms.CheckboxSelectMultiple(),
        }

    def clean_file(self):
        file = self.cleaned_data['file']
        # Limit file size to 50MB
        if file.size > 50 * 1024 * 1024:
            raise forms.ValidationError("File size must be under 50MB.")
        return file

Phase 5 — Templates

Create a templates/base.html with nav (Stories, Datasets, login/logout links using {% url %}), a {% block content %} slot, and a footer. Key templates to build:

  • stories/list.html — story grid with pagination, search form, "Has Data" badges
  • stories/detail.html — full story with author info, sources, linked datasets
  • stories/create.html — form with {% csrf_token %}, field loop, error display
  • stories/dashboard.html — editor view: stories in review, recent drafts, today's published
  • datasets/upload.html — upload form with enctype="multipart/form-data"
  • datasets/preview.html — data table showing first 50 rows of CSV
<!-- Template pattern: loop over form fields for consistent styling -->
{% for field in form %}
  <div class="form-group {% if field.errors %}has-error{% endif %}">
    <label for="{{ field.id_for_label }}">{{ field.label }}</label>
    {{ field }}
    {% if field.help_text %}<small>{{ field.help_text }}</small>{% endif %}
    {% for error in field.errors %}<p class="error">{{ error }}</p>{% endfor %}
  </div>
{% endfor %}

Template pattern: Loop over form fields for consistent styling. Each field has .label, .help_text, .errors, and .id_for_label. This keeps your template DRY. For unique layouts, render individual fields with {{ form.title }}.

Phase 6 — URL Configuration

# newsroom/urls.py — root URL config
from django.contrib import admin
from django.urls import path, include
from django.conf import settings
from django.conf.urls.static import static

urlpatterns = [
    path('admin/', admin.site.urls),
    path('', include('stories.urls')),
    path('datasets/', include('datasets.urls')),
    path('accounts/', include('accounts.urls')),
    path('api/', include('api.urls')),
]

# Serve media files in development
if settings.DEBUG:
    urlpatterns += static(settings.MEDIA_URL, document_root=settings.MEDIA_ROOT)
# stories/urls.py
from django.urls import path
from . import views

urlpatterns = [
    path('', views.story_list, name='story-list'),
    path('search/', views.story_search, name='story-search'),
    path('new/', views.story_create, name='story-create'),
    path('dashboard/', views.editor_dashboard, name='editor-dashboard'),
    path('<slug:slug>/', views.story_detail, name='story-detail'),
]
# datasets/urls.py
from django.urls import path
from . import views

urlpatterns = [
    path('', views.dataset_list, name='dataset-list'),
    path('upload/', views.dataset_upload, name='dataset-upload'),
    path('<int:pk>/', views.dataset_detail, name='dataset-detail'),
    path('<int:pk>/preview/', views.dataset_preview, name='dataset-preview'),
]

Phase 7 — Auth & Permissions

Use Django's built-in auth views for login/logout, and create a management command to set up permission groups:

# accounts/urls.py
from django.contrib.auth import views as auth_views

urlpatterns = [
    path('login/', auth_views.LoginView.as_view(template_name='accounts/login.html'), name='login'),
    path('logout/', auth_views.LogoutView.as_view(), name='logout'),
]

# stories/management/commands/setup_groups.py
# Create a management command that sets up two groups:
#   Reporters: add_story, change_story, view_story, add_dataset, view_dataset
#   Editors:   all reporter perms + delete_story, change_dataset, delete_dataset
# Run once after deployment: python manage.py setup_groups

Management commands are Django's scripts. python manage.py setup_groups runs your custom command with full ORM access. Use them for one-time setup, data seeding, or admin tasks. Run once after deployment to set up roles.

Phase 8 — REST API with DRF

# api/serializers.py
from rest_framework import serializers
from stories.models import Story, Source

class StorySerializer(serializers.ModelSerializer):
    author_name = serializers.CharField(source='author.get_full_name', read_only=True)
    class Meta:
        model = Story
        fields = ['id', 'title', 'slug', 'summary', 'status',
                  'author_name', 'created_at', 'published_at']

# api/views.py
from rest_framework import viewsets, filters
from rest_framework.permissions import IsAuthenticatedOrReadOnly

class StoryViewSet(viewsets.ModelViewSet):
    serializer_class = StorySerializer
    permission_classes = [IsAuthenticatedOrReadOnly]
    filter_backends = [filters.SearchFilter, filters.OrderingFilter]
    search_fields = ['title', 'body', 'author__first_name']

    def get_queryset(self):
        qs = Story.objects.select_related('author')
        if not self.request.user.is_authenticated:
            return qs.filter(status='published')
        return qs

    def perform_create(self, serializer):
        serializer.save(author=self.request.user)

# api/urls.py — router auto-generates all CRUD endpoints
from rest_framework.routers import DefaultRouter
router = DefaultRouter()
router.register('stories', StoryViewSet, basename='api-story')
router.register('datasets', DatasetViewSet, basename='api-dataset')
# Gives you: GET/POST /api/stories/, GET/PUT/DELETE /api/stories/5/
# Plus search: GET /api/stories/?search=election

The DRF router auto-generates RESTful URL patterns from a ViewSet. Register once, get all CRUD endpoints. The browsable API (/api/stories/ in a browser) gives you a free interactive API explorer — great for demos and debugging.

Phase 9 — Admin Configuration

# stories/admin.py
from django.contrib import admin
from .models import Story, Source

@admin.register(Story)
class StoryAdmin(admin.ModelAdmin):
    list_display = ['title', 'author', 'status', 'created_at', 'published_at']
    list_filter = ['status', 'created_at', 'author']
    search_fields = ['title', 'body']
    prepopulated_fields = {'slug': ('title',)}
    date_hierarchy = 'created_at'
    list_editable = ['status']

@admin.register(Source)
class SourceAdmin(admin.ModelAdmin):
    list_display = ['name', 'organization', 'is_verified', 'created_by']
    list_filter = ['is_verified']
    search_fields = ['name', 'organization']

The admin panel is your free CMS. With ~15 lines of code, you get a full CRUD interface with search, filters, bulk actions, and inline editing. Editors can manage stories, mark sources as verified, upload datasets — all without you building any UI. In a newsroom, this is where editors would spend most of their time during early development.

What to Show in an Interview

If you're presenting this capstone in a technical interview, here's what to emphasize and demo:

Technical Depth
  • Models: Show relationships (FK, M2M), custom methods, Meta options
  • Views: Demonstrate both FBV and CBV, explain when you'd use each
  • Forms: Custom validation, clean methods, file uploads
  • Auth: Custom user model, groups, decorators
  • API: ViewSets, serializers, filtering
  • ORM: select_related, Q objects, aggregation
Production Awareness
  • Security: CSRF, permissions, input validation
  • Performance: select_related, pagination, indexing
  • Code quality: Tests, factories, management commands
  • Deployment: Env vars, static files, Gunicorn
  • Real-world: File uploads, search, error handling
  • Domain knowledge: Newsroom workflow understanding

The "why" matters more than the "what." Don't just demo the app — explain your decisions. "I used select_related because the list page was N+1 querying for authors." "I chose a custom user model from the start because changing it later requires a database reset." "The editor dashboard uses @user_passes_test because we need role-based access, not just authentication." Show you think about tradeoffs.

Talking Points

Data modeling: Explain FK vs. M2M choices, on_delete behavior. API design: ViewSets, permissions, search. Security: CSRF, @login_required, groups. Performance: select_related, pagination. Your background: "As a journalist, I built the tool I wish I had."

Common interview mistake: Don't apologize for using Django's built-in features. "I just used ModelForm" isn't a weakness — it's a strength. Using the framework's tools effectively is exactly what a senior developer does.

Stretch Goals

If you have time: Django Channels (WebSocket notifications), D3.js frontend charts, PostgreSQL full-text search, Celery background tasks, Docker Compose, GitHub Actions CI/CD.

Check Your Understanding

Quick Quiz

1. Why should you set AUTH_USER_MODEL before running your first migration?

Django's auth system creates database tables on the first migration. If you run migrate with the default User model and then switch to a custom one, all existing migrations reference the wrong model. You'd have to delete the database and all migration files. Set it once at the start and never change it.

2. What does form.save(commit=False) do, and why use it?

commit=False creates the model instance but doesn't save to the database. This lets you add extra data (like story.author = request.user) before saving. Call .save() manually afterward. If the form has ManyToMany fields, also call form.save_m2m() after the manual save.

3. What's the difference between select_related and prefetch_related?

select_related does a SQL JOIN (one query) for ForeignKey/OneToOne relationships. prefetch_related does a separate query and joins in Python for ManyToMany and reverse ForeignKey. Both prevent N+1 query problems. Listing 20 stories that each access story.author without select_related means 21 queries instead of 1.

4. How does DRF's DefaultRouter differ from manually writing urlpatterns?

The router auto-generates all CRUD URL patterns from a ViewSet: list (GET /), create (POST /), retrieve (GET /pk/), update (PUT /pk/), delete (DELETE /pk/). Manual urlpatterns require defining each one. The router also generates a browsable API root listing all endpoints — useful for development and documentation.

5. What makes a Django management command more useful than a regular Python script?

Management commands have full access to Django's settings, ORM, and all apps — the Django environment is fully initialized. A regular Python script needs manual setup (django.setup(), environment variables). Commands integrate with manage.py, support argument parsing, and can run on deployed servers (railway run python manage.py setup_groups).
← Prev: Auth, Testing & Deployment Back to Home →
